【 Deixis 】
- The  reference  of  certain  words  and  expressions  that  relies  entirely  on  the  situational
context of  the utterance,  and can only be understood in light of these circumstances.
(1) Person deixis
- First and second-person pronouns (I,  me,  my,  mine,  we,  us,  our, you,  your, yours.) • always
| deictic (the reference is  |     |     | entirely dependent on context. )  |     |     |                                  |     |      |
| -------------------------- | --- | --- | --------------------------------- | --- | --- | -------------------------------- | --- | ---- |
|                            |     |     |                                   |     |     | Part 13. Semantics & Pragmatics  |     | 385  |

― Third-person pronouns :  deictic if they  are  free  (bound • reference is  known from context)
- this  person,  that man,  these  women,  those  children. . . .  •  deictic (they require situational
information  in  order  for  the  listener  to  make  a  referential  connection  and  understand
what is meant.
(2)  Time  deixis
— now,  this time,  then,  that time,  tomorrow,  seven days ago,  last week,  next April ...
- To  understand  what  specific  times  expressions  refer  to,  we  need  to  know  when  the
utterance was said.
(3) Place deixis
| - here,  there,  | this place,  | this  city,  those  | towers over there ...  |     |     |

| 학 중에서도 cataphoric  | words나 hedges같은  |     |     |     | 전체적으로  |
| ------------------ | ---------------- | --- | --- | --- | ------ |
봤을 때는 대화 속에서,  특정 context에서 자신이  의도한 것을 나타내기  위해 사용한  언어를 분석하는  내용
‘ ’
이므로 pragmatics로 볼 수 있다.
4.  해설:  S1의  "Oh,  wait,  on  her  jacket,  I  found  this.  There  is  a  letter  P  on  it.”을  보면this 라는
대명사를  먼저  이야기한  다음에  그  ‘this’가  의미하는  ‘a  letter  P'는  그  뒤에  이어져  나온다.  이는
'catapho’ric  words’에  해당된다.  （보통의  순서는  나타내는  대상이  먼저  나오고,  대명사가  나중에  나오는
데  이는  anaphoric  reference이다)  그리고  "He  is  in  a  blue  coat.  It  is  sort  of  neat"문장을  보면
'sort  of라는  표현을  쓰면서  ‘neat'한  것  같긴  한데  다소  정확하지만은  않다는  (명확히  이야기하는  것
을  피하기  위해)  것을  표현하고  있다.  이는  ‘hedges’에  해당된다.  (TG를  보면  hedges가 stance  adverb인
| sort  of,  kind of와 같은 표현을 사용한다는 말이  |     |     | 있다.)  |     |     |
| ------------------------------------ | --- | --- | ----- | --- | --- |
[A nswet]
First,  a  cataphoric  word  'this'  is  used  in 81's utterance  "Oh,  wait,  on her jacket,  I  found
this.  There  is  a letter P on it",  in  which the identity of 'this'  is  established by the following
sentence.  Second,  'hedges'  are  used  in 82's utterance "It is sort of neat".  By using the  hedge
| 'sort of',  82 avoids making a definite statement.  |     |     |     |     |     |
| --------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
386  2.  영어학

홉
프 기출분석집
� Concept Check
✓ hedges: Stance adverbs such as sort of, kind of, and roughly are referred to as hedges
because they express a degree of imprecision and allow the speaker to avoid making a
definitive statement (e. g. I aint' seen this series. I just sort of remember from the last
series.)
✓ Back-channel cues: verbalization speakers make to show that they're listening. (e. g.
mhm, uhuh, yeah, I see ....) These types of signals provide feedback to the current speaker
that the message is being received
✓ Anaphoric reference: when definite articles or pronouns indicate identity of reference with
that established earlier in the discourse
✓ Cataphoric reference: when the identity of the reference will be established by what follows

2. 출제의도/해설 long과 short은 antonym중에서도 gradable에  속한댜  단어가 절대적은 수치를 나타내지는
않고  문맥마다 달라질 수  있으며  둘 사이에  middle gr” oup의  단어도 올 수  있다.  （예.  hot- 'warm'-cold)

【 Answet 】
Between the gradable pair of antonyms 'long' and 'short',  'long' is unmarked because it is usually
used in questions of degree.

【 Antonym 】
- one of a pair of words with opposite meanings
| (1) Complementary Pairs  | (Binary Antonymy)  |     |     |
| ------------------------ | ------------------ | --- | --- |
- Denial of one term is  the  assertion of its  complementary term  • X is not Y and Y is  not X
- X  or Y  •  No middle group
- alive-dead,  present-absent,  awake-asleep,  single-married,  true-false,  pass-fail. ..
(2) Relational opposites
| - relationship between  | two  items from opposite perspectives.  |     |     |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------- | --- | --- |
| - display  symmetry     | in their meaning                        |     |     |
- Reversal verbs (give-receive,  lend-borrow,  buy-self) ,  relationship (husband-wife,  teacher-
students,  employer-employee,  trainer-trB.I·nee),  position in  time  and space (in front of-be坤.nd,
| outside-inside,  above-below)  |     |     |     |
| ------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- |
(3) Gradable pairs
| - describe opposite ends of a  | continuous dimension  |     |     |
| ------------------------------ | --------------------- | --- | --- |
- do not provide an absolute scale (the norm varies from context to context)
| - permit intermediate stages  | (멘 聖1- | warm -cool- | 꼬뵤)  |
| ----------------------------- | ------ | ----------- | ---- |
- big-small,  hot-cold,  fast-slow,  happy-sad,  wet-drY,  many-few,  good-bad,  beautiful-ugly, ..
| - unmarked member is  | the  one used     | in  questions of degree  |            |
| --------------------- | ----------------- | ------------------------ | ---------- |
| 몐 How high(not low)   | is the mountain?  | ('high' :                | unmarked)  |

1. 분류: Semantics • Truth condi tt ii on •  Presupposition  10
i
2.  출제의도/해설:  Presupposition에  대한  문제가  나왔다.  이  때  이후로  년  이상 나오지  않고  있지만
Semantics원서에서 많이 언급되는 개념이므로  언젠가 나올  것  같다.  Presupposition은 하나의  문장에서
’
world / background knowledge를 동원하면 당연히  알 수  있는 내용이다.  예를  들어  “I  regret that she
란
lost  the  race. ’라는  문장에서  내가 후회  했든  안  했든  "She  lost  the  race" 사실은  전제되어있다는
|     |           |     | 알 수  | 있고,    |     | presupposition이다.  |     |         |              |     |
| --- | --------- | --- | ---- | ------ | --- | ------------------ | --- | ------- | ------------ | --- |
|     | 것을 상식적으로  |     |      | 이것이 그  |     | 문장의  ”             |     | 이  내용은  | 이미 전제가 되어있기  |     |
때문에  main clause를 부정해도 presupposition은 꼭 살아있어야 한다.  "I  don't regret that she lost
’
the race." 이라고 부정해도 "she lost the race 는 여전히  presupposition으로 남아있다.  이것을 'Constancy
under negation 이라고 하고 presupposition의  가장 큰 특징이다.  （이는 Entailment,  implicative verb에는
’
적용되지  않기  때문에  셋을 구분할 때  좋은 특징이다.  특히  Entailment는 A가  B를  entail한다고  해도
’
|     | A의   | B를 entail하는  |     |                  |     |     | presupposition을 유발하는 'regret |     |     | 과       |
| --- | ---- | ------------ | --- | ---------------- | --- | --- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- | ------- |
|     | 부정이  |              |     | 것은 아니다.） 또한 이렇게  |     |     |                              |     |     | 같은 동사를  |
|     |      |              |     |                  |     |     | 와                            | 써   |     |         |
'presupposition trigger 라고 부른댜  (think,  suppose 같은 단어를 대신  보면  ’she lost the  race를
볼
여전히  presuppose하는 constancy under negation이  적용되지 않는  것을  수  있다.） 이  문제에서도
presupposition trigger를 찾으면 된다.  간단하게  적용할 수 있는 것은 main clause를 부정해 보는 것인데,
부정해도 종속절이 사실로  전제되는 문장은  (a),  (d),  (e)이며 여기서의 angry,  surprised,  significant가
|     | presupposition trigger |     |     | 이   |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | ---------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
다.

390  2.  영어학

기출분석집
【 Presupposition 】
(1) Definition :  an  implicit assumption about the world or background belief relating to an
| utterance whose truth is taken for  |     |     |     |     | granted in discourse  |     |     |     |
| ----------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
(2) Constancy under negation:  presupposition survives even when the main clause is negated.
·  I  regret that Jane failed  again �  (presupposes) � Jane failed again
·  I  don't regret that Jane failed again �  (presupposes)  �  Jane failed again
| (3)  |     |     |     | If  |     |     |     |     |
| ---- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Entailment  vs  Presupposition :  we  negate  an  entailing  sentence  ’  then  the  entailment
fails(it  can  be  either  true  or  false) ;  but  negating  a  presupposing  sentence  allows  the
presupposition to survive.
| ·   | ' The president  | was assassinated  |     |     | entails ' The president is dead'  |     |     |     |
| --- | ---------------- | ----------------- | --- | --- | --------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- |
·  'The president  was not assassinated' does NOT  entail  'The president is dead'

、

structure or word that pre supposes the truth of the proposition.
(4) Presupposition triggers:
1〉 factive  verbs  (significant,  odd,  tragic,  exciting,  relevant,  matter,  count,  amuse,  bother,
| surprise,  | realize. .. )  |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ---------- | -------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
·  It is � that Paul is proud of himself � (presupposes) � Paul is proud of himself.
|     | ·  John realized that he was  |     |     | in  | debt.  | � John was in fact  |     | in  debt  |
| --- | ----------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | ------ | ------------------- | --- | --------- |
2〉 Definite expression
·  John  saw the man with  two heads.  �  there  exists  a  man with  two heads.
3〉 Implicative verbs
|     | ·  John � to  |     | open the door.  |     | �   | John  tried to open  |     | the door.  |
| --- | ------------- | --- | --------------- | --- | --- | -------------------- | --- | ---------- |
·  John 노 to lock the door.  � John  ought  to have locked,  or intended to lock,  the door.
4〉 Change of state verbs  (stop,  begin, start,  finish,  carry on,  leave ... )
|     | ·  John � teasing his wife.  |     |     |     | � John had been  |     | teasing his wife.  |     |
| --- | ---------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | ---------------- | --- | ------------------ | --- |
·  Joan � teasing her husband. � Joan hadn't been teasing her husband.
5〉 Iteratives (anoth er time  to  come back,  restore,  repeat,  for  the nth  time.  .)
|     | ·  The flying saucer came 뿌로프.  |     |     |     | �   | The flying saucer came before.  |     |     |
| --- | ------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | ------------------------------- | --- | --- |
·  You can't get gobstoppers �- �  You once could get gobstoppers.
6〉 Cleft  sentences
·  Cleft  construction:  � Henry 1hfil kissed Rosie.  �  Someone kiss�d Rosie.
·  Pseudo-cleft  construction:  � John lost 짝!.§. his wallet.  � John lost something,

392  2.  영어학

기출분석집
- Lexical Entailment : Hyponymy between lexical items is a regular source for entailment
between sentences.
뗀 I bought a dog today -+ (entails) -+ I bought an animal today.
(dog is a hyponym of animal)
- Syntactic Entailment : active and passive versions of the same sentence will entail one
another.
뗀 My brother built this building -+ (entails) -+ This building was built by my brother.
-
- If two sentences mutually entail each other, we call it 'paraphrase'
1. 분류: Semantics • Truth condition • Implicative verb
2. 출제의도/해설: Truth condition에는 Entailment, presupposition 두 가지가 유명하지만 Implicative
’
verbs도 역시 중요하다. Implicative verb란 동사 그 자체에 특정한 추론이 담겨있는 동사들이다. 예를
들어, 'manage to-’는 부정사절을 '하긴 했다 라는 positive implication을 담고 있다. 그러나 이런 동사
들은 presupposition의 factive verb와는 다르게, 부정을 하면 그 implication의 결과가 바뀌게 된다.
(didn't manage to- 라고 하면 결국 못 한 것이다) 본문에서는 manage와 try가 나와있다. manage는
언급한대로 긍정으로 쓰이면 긍정, 부정으로 쓰이면 부정의 implication이 있기 때문에 긍정으로 쓰인
(3)의 문장이 뒤에서 부정되어서 모순이 있었다. 반면 try는 긍정으로 쓰일 때는 no implication이지만
(했을 수도 있고, 아닐 수도 있다) 부정으로 쓰이면 부정의 implication이기 때문에 긍정으로 쓰인 (4)의
문장은 뒤에 긍정 / 부정 중 어느 것이 쓰여도 모순이 되지는 않는다. implicative verb는 연습문제에 더
자세히 정리를 해 놓았다.
【 An5Wer 】
As the verb 'manage' in the affirmative sentence implies the fulfilled action of answering
the question, the following clause "and finally she answered it" is redundant and unnecessary,

1. 분류: Semantics  •  Information Structure  •  Focus structure (Cleft sentence)
2.  출제의도/해설:  Focus  structure에  관한  내용이다.  Grammar파트에도  들어갈 수  있고,  Semantics 파트
에도  들어갈  수  있다.  특히  이  문제는 의미적인 내용에  초점을 맞추어서 답을  할  것을  요구하고  있다.
Cleft  sentence를 사용하는  이유는  It - that 사이에  Focus  (prominence)를 줄 단어를  넣기  위한  것이다.
〈B 의  (a)~(c)는 It과 that 사이 에  a  meteorite,  last week,  on  the  roof라는 문맥상의  새로 언급
되는〉  new information을  넣었지만, 각(d각)의    Sally는  질문에서  이미  나왔던 old information이고 강조구문
| 에서 Focus를 둘  | 없었다.  |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------ | ----- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
필요가
394  2.  영어학

홉
도 기출분석집
【 Answer 】
(d) is inappropriate. In cleft sentences like (a)~(c), the element (constituent) located between
"It" and "that" is given a prominent focus, providing new information. In (d) , however, 'Sally' is
old information already mentioned in the previous sentence, so it doesn't need to be focused.
【 Focus structure 】
The prominent part of the sentence is usually called the 'focus'. One function of focus is to
—
mark new information. Certain constructions serve to place parts of the sentence in focus,
known as clefts in (1) and the pseudo cleft in (2), where the focus elements are underlined.
(la) It was � that Louis came.
(lb) It was � who came yesterday.
(2) What we want is �
In (la) and (lb) , the basic situation described is the same : Louis came yesterday, but the
information is packaged differently to fit different states of participants' knowledge at the
specific point in the conversation.

\ # 2018.A.9
1. 분류 : Semantics • The structure of meaning • Scope Ambiguity
(Syntax • Ambiguity • Scope ambiguity)
2. 키워드: Scope Ambiguity, quantifier, scope of negation
3. 출제의도/ 해설
Scope ambiguity에 대한 문제가 나왔다. Ambiguity는 Syntax 영역에도 들어가고 Semantics 영역에
도 들어가는 파트인데 Scope Ambiguity는 Semantics에서 더 많이 다루긴 한다. 지금까지 기출에서는
lexical, structural ambiguity가 출제되어서 생소한 개념일 수는 있었지만 ambiguity 해석 쓰는 연습을
하고, sco’pe of negation등을 공부해 놓았다면 지문의 정보로 답을 추론은 할 수 있게 출제가 되었다.
영어의 all, any, every, many, much, some과 같은 단어들을 ‘Quantifier' 라고 한댜 이는 말 그대
로 ‘수량을 정해주는 말인데, 문장 내에서 그 수량을 정해주는 범위 (scope)에 따라 해석이 달라질
수 있다. 특히 두 개 이상의 quantifier가 같이 쓰인 경우는 각각의 의미상의 범위에 따라 중의적인 의
미를 가져올 수도 있는데 이를 scope a’mbiguity라고 한다. 두 가지 quantifier가 있을 경우, 더 넓은 쪽
의 의미가 중요하니 문장의 앞으로 빼고 해석하는 것이 좋다. (la)의 예시에서 Every의 범위가 더 wide
한 경우엔 every의 범위 안에 ‘a girl 이 들어’ 가게 되는데, 이 때 Every의 의미가 문장 전체에 영향을
끼치며 "Every boy가 있는데, 그 모든 소년들이 각각 좋아하는 소녀가 있다(Every boy /has a gir1
who they like)" 의 해석이 나온다. ‘a girl 의 범위가 더 넓은 경우엔 그 안에 every가 들어가게 되
고, A girl의 의미가 문장 전체에 영향을 끼치며 "A girl이 있는데, 모든 남자들이 좋아한다. (There is

the  case  that  they  have  done  their  assignment) 의  해석이댜  Not이  더  큰  경우엔  Not을  앞으로

이댜  즉,  Not이  나머지  문장을  부정하며  “아니다,  모든  학생이  숙제를 한  것은  (It  is  not  the  case
that  every  student  that  has  done  their  assignment_)"  의  해석이다.  그리고 이  때  Not이  Every를

‘visit  none  of  the  city',  'not  visit  any  city'등이  표현으로  쉽게  쓰는  것이  좋을  것  같
큰  경우엔
고 refuse가 큰 경우엔 ‘visit some  cities (but not  all cities)'  와 같은 표현을  적으면 좋다.
【 Answer 】
Two  possible  meanings  are  "Mary  did  not  visit  any  city  that  Tom  visited. "  and  "Mary  visited
some  cities  that  Tom  visited  but  not  all  the  cities. ,,  In  case  of  the  first  meaning,  ·every
city'  has  a  wide  scope  over  negative  expression  'refuse'.  For  the  second  meaning,
refuse· has a wide scope over the ·every city'.
396  2.  영어학

홉
도 기출분석집
Semantics 2nd edit10n
【 Scope Ambiguity 】
We can take as an example the way that representation of quantifiers clarifies some
ambiguities found in natural languages. One of these is scope ambiguity, which can occur when
(le) .
there is more than one quantifier in a sentence. For example, the English sentence (la) below
has the two interpretations paraphrased in (lb) and
(1) a. Everyone loves someone.
b. Everyone has someone that they love.
c. There is some person who is loved by everyone.
Version (lb) involves a many-to-many relationship of loving, while version (lc) involves a
many-to-one relationship. While the English sentence is structurally ambiguous between these
two interpretations, the difference is explicitly shown in predicate logic by the ordering of
quantifiers.
In (lb), the universal quantifier (='all' and 'every') comes leftmost and therefore contains
(le),
the existential quantifier (='some') in its scope. This situation is described as the universal
quantifier having wide scope. In we have the reverse: the existential quantifier contains
the universal in its scope and therefore takes wide scope. Thus , the scope of one quantifier
may be contained within the scope of another.
Negative words, like English not, also display scope over a predication and a second advantage
of this type of representation is that it allows us to disambiguate some sentences which
contain combinations of quantifiers and negation. The sentence (2a) , for example, can have the
two interpretations (2b, 2c) .
(2) a. Everybody didn't visit Limerick.
b. For every person x, it's not the case that x visited Limerick.
c. It's not the case that every person x visited Limerick.
In (2b) , the universal quantifier has wide scope over the negative word, while in (2c) , the
negative has wide scope over the universal quantifier.

【 Word  Formation 】
| (1)  Coinage:     | types of brand products •  |                                                 | used as  the generic name.  |     |
| ----------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- | --- |
| 뗀 Xerox,          | Kleenex, Aspirin,          | Google,                                         | Zipper ...                  |     |
| (2) Compounding:  |                            | two or more words are joined to form new words  |                             |     |
몐 sleep / walk, land / lord, bitter / sweet,  easy / going, crest / fallen, out / run, high / light ...
(3) Blending:  Similar to compounds but only parts of the words are combined.
멘 smog (smoke+fog) ,  brunch(breakfast+lunch) ,  heliport(helicopter+ airport) ,
bromance(brother+romance) ,  Globish (global+English) ,  textpectation(text message+
expectation)
(4) Clipping:  abbreviation of longer words into shorter ones (without changing meaning)
| 몐 fax(facsimile),  |     | piano(pianoforte),  | ad,  bike,  math,  | gas,  phone  |
| ------------------ | --- | ------------------- | ------------------ | ------------ |
(5) Back-formation:  the word formation process in which an actual or supposed derivational affix
| detaches from the base form of the word to create a  |     |     |     | new word.  |
| ---------------------------------------------------- | --- | --- | --- | ---------- |
- back-formation  is  often  the  result  of  an  overgeneralization  of  derivation  suffixes.
| (incorrect morphological analysis  |     |     | •  creates new word)  |     |
| ---------------------------------- | --- | --- | --------------------- | --- |
- back-formation may change the part of speech or the word's meaning,  whereas clipping creates
shortened  words  from  longer  words,  but  does  not  change  the  part  of  speech  or  the
―
meaning of the word.
몐 peddle� peddle,  edito�edit,  stoke�stoke,  television국elevise,  resurrection resurrect,
(6) C
translation� translate
onversion :  the  word-form remains same  but it realizes a  different lexical  item (to a  new
syntactic category)
몐 experience (verb to noun),  email  (noun to verb),  green (adjective to verb),  up  / down
| (preposition to noun as in  |     | ups and downs of life)  |     |     |
| --------------------------- | --- | ----------------------- | --- | --- |
(7)  Derivation:  the forming of new words by prefixation or suffixation
- prefixation :  antifreeze(anti+freeze) ,  biannual(bi+annual) ,  atypical(a+typical)
- suffixation :  drinkable(drink+able) ,  trustee(trust+ee) ,  kindness(kind+ness)
| 1  1   | J i l | i   J  |     |     |
| ------ | ----- | ------ | --- | --- |
[ ＃
| 2006서울5              |     | # 2008전국18:                          |     |     |
| -------------------- | --- | ------------------------------------ | --- | --- |
| 1. 분류: Morphology •  |     | the hierarchical structure of words  |     |     |
2.  해설  prefix나 suffix가 stem에 붙어  새로운 단어를 만드는 derivation에  대한 설명이  나오고 있다.  이렇게
품사를  바꾸거나 의미를 바꾸는 morpheme은  derivational  morpheme이라고  한다.  단어에  2개  이상의
derivational morpheme이  붙는  경우에는 정해진 순서가  있거나,  순서에  따라  의미가 달라질 수  있다.

,빠
| 1. 분류: Morphology  |     | ••  constraints on  | productivity  | -en suffix  |     |     |     |
| ------------------ | --- | ------------------- | ------------- | ----------- | --- | --- | --- |
2.  출제의도/해설:  지문을  읽어보면 단어의  base에  affix가 붙는  derivation에  대한 설명이 나오고,  그  예로
형용사를  동사로  만드는 -en  suffix가  붙는 조건을  설명하고  있다.  첫째는 monosyIlabic이어야  하고,
둘째는  obstruent  (stop,  fricative,  affricate)  로  끝나야  한다는  것이다.  이  조건에  맞지  않는  단어는
abstracten (단음절 이  아님 )  greenen, bluen (obstruent로 끝나지  않고 sonorant로 끝남) 이 다.
| 3.  정 답  | abstracten,  | greenen,  bluen  |     |     |     |     |     |
| -------- | ------------ | ---------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
[  ＃
서술
| 2014.                 |             | 5  :                 |               |             |         |     |     |
| --------------------- | ----------- | -------------------- | ------------- | ----------- | ------- | --- | --- |
| 1. 분류 :               | Morphology  | •  Constraints on    | productivity  | •  -al      | suffix  |     |     |
| 2.  키워드: -al suffix.  |             | anterior,  coronal,  | sonorant,     | continuant  |         |     |     |
3.  출제의도:  phonetics&phonology와  morphology의  결합문제이다.  주관식  시절 -en  suffix가  나왔었고
이번엔  다뤄질  것  같지  않아서  수험생들이  별로 신경을 안 쓰던 _al suffix가 출제되었다.  suffix -en과
_al은 보통 morphology에서 다뤄지기는 한댜  시험 유형이  바뀌면서 문제  수가  많이  줄었기  때문에  이런
식으로 두 가지  영역을 다 물어볼 수 있는 문제를 유의해서 봐야 할 듯하다.  이런 유형의  문제는 예전에는
규칙을 주어주고,  예시  중  적절한  것이나 적절하지  않은 것을 고르는 문제였지만 예시를 보고 룰을 도
출하라는  inductive한  방식으로  물어봤기  때문에  낯설고  어려웠던  문제였다.  지문에서는  주어진
distinctive feature가  가장 큰  힌트다.  두  가지  표가  나와있는데,  출제자의  의도를  눈치  재고  처음으로
나와  있는 anterior와  coronal을  Requirement  2에  적용하여서  그  두  기준을  중심으로  정문인  예시와
|             |     | 차이를 분석하고,  | 같은 방법으로 sonorant,  |     | continuant를 Requirement  |     | 3로    |
| ----------- | --- | ---------- | ------------------ | --- | ------------------------ | --- | ----- |
| 비문인 예시 사이의  |     |            |                    |     |                          |     | 적용하면  |
그나마 답을 도출하기  쉬워진다.  이제 몇  년간 -al suffix가 다시  나올 확률은 적지만  이런 식으로  예시를
주고 규칙을 도출하는 문제는 나올 수 있다.
4.  해설
- Requirement 2:  -al  suffix 가 허용되는 세  단어의  마지막 자음 th[히,  v[v],  t[t]를 보면 dental 아니면
alveolar이라서  [+anterior]이다.  그러나  비문인  세  단어의  끝을  보면  k[k],  ch[tf]로  끝나서  palato­
alveolar아니면 Velar sound이라서  [―anterior]이다.  즉,  －al이  deverbal suffix로  쓰여서  동사에  붙을
| 때(동사에 붙어서  |     |              |         | 마지막 자음은 anterior sound여야만 한다.  |     |     |     |
| ---------- | --- | ------------ | ------- | ------------------------------ | --- | --- | --- |
|            |     | 명사를  만들 때)，  | 그  동사의  |                                |     |     |     |
- Requirement 3:  Requirement 2에서 이미 마지막 consonant가 anterior가 와야 한다는 것을 알았다.
|      | 여기서는 -al suffix가 붙기  |     |           | [t],[s]와  |               | sound로  |             |
| ---- | -------------------- | --- | --------- | --------- | ------------- | ------- | ----------- |
| 그래서  |                      |     | 전  동사는 다  |           | 같은  anterior  |         | 끝난다.  그렇다면  |
다른 조건을 봐야  한다.  보통 suffix가 붙는 조건을 따질  때  suffix  바로  앞의  소리의  성격이 같다면
그  소리의  하나  더  앞을 봐야  한다.  그렇다면  Requirement  2와는  달리  anterior  consonant  앞에
|     |     |     |     |     |     | Part 14.  | Morphology  401  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --------- | ---------------- |

----- _....1_fflt.ltlla1...__ __________________________________ ____ __ __ __ __ __ __ _ -- -------------------------________ _
| consonant가  |            |          | 있을 것이다.  | 그  자음을 지문의  | 표와 비교하며  | 잘 보면 된다.  |     |
| ----------- | ---------- | -------- | -------- | ----------- | -------- | --------- | --- |
|             | 하나  더  있는  | 것을  알 수  |          |             |          |           |     |
nt,  rs,  rs와 같이
| 허용되는 단어의  | 마지막 두 자음을 보면  |     |     | 끝에서  두  | 번째  자음이  | [+sonorant]인  | 반면,  |
| --------- | ------------- | --- | --- | ------- | -------- | ------------- | ---- |
허용되지  않은  단어의  두  자음을  보면 pt,  st,  ft와  같이  끝에서  두  번째  자음이  [-sonorant]이다.
즉,  -al suffix 동사에 붙을 때  그 동사가 두 개의 자음으로 끝난다면  [+sonorant]와 [+anterior]  순서 로
와야 한댜
’
(사실 끝에 두 자음이  +sonorant와 -sonorant 순서라고 해도 Requirement 3의  예시들이  설명이 된다.
그러나 지문에 'deverbal suffix -al은 다음 3개의 조건을 만족해야한다 라는 말이  있고,  이미 Requirement  1
은 지문에  제시를 했기  때문에 Requirement 2와 3를  연계해서 적는 것이 좋을 것  같아서  [+anterior]
를  기본으로  두고  그  앞에  [+sonorant]를  추가하는  식의 설명이 좋다.  실제  원서에서도 이런 식으로
| 설명하】고  있다.)  |     |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| ------------ | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
【 Answer
As  for  Requirement 2,  deverbal suffix -al  attaches only to the verbs that end with an anterior
consonant.  Besides,  regarding  Requirement  3,  if  two  consecutive  consonants  precede  the  -al
suffix,  a  consonant that  precedes  an anterior consonant must be sonorant.  (the first  consonant
of the cluster must be sonorant)
| 【   | 】   |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Deverbal suffix -aI
Suffix —al  can  be  attached to certain verbs to give nouns.  The  condition is  that the  verb must
have stress on its final syllable and may have no more than two consonants at the end,  of which
the  last  must  be  anterior  and  the  next-to-last  must  be  a  sonorant.  This  condition  can  be
溫
| stated formaIl ly b[y   | ＋ s ： ub 麟 categ]or（iza［t i | on fra]m）e （lik［e  :溫  |     | ]）  |     |     |     |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------- | ---------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
-al]N
|     |     |     |     | ]v _  |     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | ----- | --- | --- | --- |
Examples of verbs that meet tI his condition are given in
|                  |                        |          |          | the  below.  | alveolar       |            |     |
| ---------------- | ---------------------- | -------- | -------- | ------------ | -------------- | ---------- | --- |
| vowel            | final                  |          | labials  |              |                |            |     |
|                  |                        | I        |          | procure,     | appraise,      | propose,   |     |
| deny,  try,      | withdraw, I retrieve,  |          | arrive,  | survive,     |                |            |     |
|                  |                        |          |          | dispose,     | rehearse,      | disperse,  |     |
| renew,  betray,  | portray                | remove,  | approve  |              |                |            |     |
|                  |                        |          |          | deverse.     | rent.  acquit  |            |     |
That the final consonant must be [+anterior]  can be seen from the impossibility of  *begrudgeal,
*rebukal,  *impeachal,  *encroachal,  and  *detachal.  That the vowel in the last syllable must be
stressed is seen from the impossibility of  *promissal,  *abandonal,  *developal,  and  *edital.

’
- 본 자료는 ‘루이스 기출분석 의 각 분류에서 출제된 개출 개념 위주로 모은 것입니다. 모든 개념이 포

r
# Part1 . Second Language Acquisition ]
【1】 Theories / models of Second Language Acquisition
1 p
. Perspectives on second language acquisition
(1) Structural Linguistics and Behavioral Psychology: examine only publicly(overtly) observable
response, ignore any mental process, language could be dismantled into small pieces or
units and that these unites could be described scientifically, contrasted and added up again
to form the whole.
(2) Cognitive psychology: a branch of psychology that deals with such processes as attention,
perception, comprehension, memory, and learning. In contrast with behaviourism, cognitive
psychology is concerned with mental processes and the representation of knowledge in the
mind.
(3) Constructivism: a social and educational philosophy based on the beliefs that: 1) knowledge
is actively constructed by learners and not passively received, 2) cognition is an adaptive
process that organizes the learner's experiential world, 3) all knowledge is socially
constructed
(4) Sociocultural viewpoint: Sociocultural viewpoints differed from cognitivist perspectives in
their focus on interaction, as opposed to the individual learner, and on language as the
major tool for engaging in collaborative activity in a community of language users.
(5) Zone of Proximal Development(ZPD) : the metaphorical distance between a learner's
existing developmental state and his or her potential development/ the domain of knowledge
or skill where the learner is not yet capable of independent functioning, but can achieve
the desired outcome given relevant scaffolded help,
2. Input hypothesis : the idea that exposure to comprehensible input which contains structures
that are slightly in advance of a learner's current level of competence is the necessary and
sufficient cause of second language acquisition.
(1) comprehensible input is necessary for language acquisition • comprehensible input
contains structure one step more advanced than the current stage of learners'
interlanguage
(2) Affective filter hypothesis : the best acquisition will occur in environment where anxiety is
Part 1. Second Language Acquisition 415

__ft M+e_ _____________________
low and defensiveness absent
(3) natural order hypotheses : we acquire language rules in a predictable or 'natural' order
(4) acquisition-learning hypothesis
(5) monitor hypothesis
3. Interaction hypothesis: the hypothesis that language acquisition requires or greatly benefits
from interaction, communication and especially negotiation of meaning, which happens when
interlocutors attempt to overcome problems in conveying their meaning, resulting in both
additional input and useful feedback on the learner's own production.
(1) acquisition is facilitated when 1〉 interactional modifications lead to comprehensible input,
2〉 learners receive feedback, 3〉 learners are pushed to reformulate their own utterances
(2) negotiation of meaning: 1〉 helps to resolve communication breakdowns and to work toward
mutual comprehension, 2〉 help Ss to obtain comprehensible input, 3〉 prompts Ss to
adjust, manipulate and modify their own output ( Ss are pushed into producing more
comprehensible output)
(3) Negotiation sequence : trigger • indicator • response • reaction
4. Output hypothesis: the hypothesis that successful second language acquisition requires not
only comprehensible input, but also comprehensible output, language produced by the learner
that can be understood by other speakers of the language.
(1) Pushed output : output that reflects what learners can produce when they are pushed to use
the target language accurately and concisely. It occurs when Ss experience communicative
failure and they are forced to make their output more coherent.
(2) Modified output: language produced by the learner that is modified from the initial
utterance either in response to feedback or without feedback, irrespective of the extent to
which the reformulation is targetlike
(3) Three functions of output :
一 Noticing function : Learners encounter gaps between what they want to say and what
they are able to say (consciously recognize linguistic shortcoming)
― Hypothesis국esting function : the learner tests a hypothesis of how to say their intent
- Metalinguistic function: Learners reflect on the language they learn, and thereby the
output enables them to control and internalize linguistic knowledge
5 . Language and Culture
(1) Language competence
1〉 Organizational competence • Grammatical competence, Textual (discourse) competence
a. Grammatical competence: focuses on sentence-level grammar
b. Discourse competence
416 3. 부 록

홉
도그: 71출분석집
- the ability to connect sentences in stretches of discourse and to form a meaningful
빠
whole out
- focuses on intersentential relationships deals with Cohesion, Coherence
2〉 Pragmatic competence : knowledge that people must have in order to interpret and convey
meaning within communicative situation
a. illocutionary competence: sending/receiving intended meanings
b. sociolinguistic competence: the extent to which second language can be used or
understood appropriately in various contexts to convey specific communicative functions
such as describing, persuading, . . • appropriateness of the attitude, choice of style or
register, formality, cultural aspects. ..
―
빠
* Register: vary the choice of vocabulary, syntax, pronunciation, intonation to tailor message
for a particular person on social context requires sensitivity to_ cross cultural
differences
* Formality: variations of linguistic style or tone in relation to conventions regarding
appropriateness to the social context of use: in relation to the situation, setting
(public or private), communicative genre, audience size, and communicative
relationships
Cf) Strategic competence : use of effective verbal/nonverbal communication strategies to
compensate for gaps in language knowledge or for breakdown in
communication
(2) World Englishes: A term for emerging localized or indigenized varieties of English • deals
with varieties of English used in diverse sociolinguistic contexts globally and analyzing how
sociolinguistic histories, multicultural bac�grounds and contexts of function influence the
use of English in different regions of the world.
(3) Speech acts: Ss should learn the appropriate ways to accomplish their goals when they are
speaking • This includes thanking, requesting information, apologizing, refusing, warning,
complimenting, directing, complaining and so on. � different forms can be used to
accomplish the same speech act.
(4) Proxemics : the study of the physical distance between people when they are talking to each
other, as well as their postures and whether or not there is physical contact during their
conversation. These factors can be looked at in relation to the sex, age, and social and
cultural background of the people involved, and also their attitudes to each other and their
state of mind.
(5) Euphemism: a word or phrase that replaces a taboo word or is used to avoid frightening or
unpleasant subjects.
(6) lntercultural competence : being able to understand the differences which may arise as a
result of different cultural backgrounds and to communicate and integrate across these
differences

【2】 Error Analysis
[A] Source/types of the error
1. Source of the error
(1) interference
- Negative transfer: the learner's 11 is one of the sources of error in learner language.
- Positive transfer: the learner's 11 can facilitate 12 acquisition.
- Contrastive Analysis: The source of error is 11-12 differences • compare 11-12
differences • predict easy/difficult areas • teach what is dissimilar
(2) overgeneralization: the pprroocceessss ooff ggeenneerraalliizzi i ng a particular rule or item in the second
language, irrespective of the native language beyond conventional rules or boundaries
2 . Types of errors
(1) Global errors : cause problems in communication and include errors such as wrong word
order to inappropriate uses of lexical items.
(2) Local errors : do not affect general understanding of the message and usually have to do
with minor errors such as those related to the omission of morphological markers or
function words.
cf〉 Overt error: unquestionably ungrammatical at the sentence level (=sentence level error)
Covert error: grammatically well-formed at the sentence level but are not interpretable
within the context of communication. (=discourse level error)
[BJ lnterlanguage
- a transitional point between learner's 11 system and target language system.
- systematic, variable, dynamic
- 4 stages : presystematic • emergent • systematic • stabilization
Restructuring: a qualitative change as the learner moves from stage to stage in
development
[C] Error correction feedback
1. Types of Error correction feedback
(1) Clarification request: indicates to Ss either that their utterance has been misunderstood by
T or that the utterance is ill-formed in some way and that a repetition or a reformulation
is required. (I'm sorry.끼
418 3. 부 록

홉
도그: 71출분석집
(2) Metalinguistic feedback: provides comments, information, or questions related to the well­
formedness of the Ss' utterance. (remember the present perfect tense.끼
(3) Elicitation: prompts S to self-correct. ( What does. .. . 꺼
(4) Explicit correction: a clear indication to S that the form is incorrect (No, not 'have : say
was')
(5) Repetition: T repeats the ill-formed part of the S's utterance ucsually with a change in
intonation.
(6) Recast: reformulate an ill-formed utterance in an unobtrusive way
cf) Confirmation checks : Moves by which one speaker seeks confirmation of the other's
preceding utterance through repetition, with rising intonation, of what was perceived to
be all or part of the preceding utterance.
Comprehension checks: Moves by which one speaker attempts to determine whether the
other speaker has understood a preceding message.
cf) Affective feedback (gesture, tone, facial expression)
Cognitive feedback (linguistic devices • sounds, phrases, structures, discourse)
cf) positive evidence: well-formed sentences to which learners are exposed
negative evidence: information that is provided to S s concerning the incorrectness of an
utterance
cf) foreigner talk : the type of speech often used by native speakers of a language when speaking
to foreigners who are not proficient in the language.
2. Teacher's question type
(1) Display Q: T or the questioner already knows the response or has a very limited set of
expectations for the appropriate response
(2) Referential Q: T or the questioner does not know beforehand the response information.
3. Uptake: a student's immediate verbal reaction to T's feedback (that constitutes a reaction in
some way to T's intention to draw attention to some aspect of S's initial utterance.)
(1) Successful uptake : Ss incorporates the linguistic information into his/her subsequent
production.
S : Sometimes that our students leader will keep the will keep the, er.
T: Order
S: Order.
(2) Unsuccessful uptake : Ss' failure to incorporate, fully or partially, into his/her production
the linguistic information that the feedback was intended to address. (but Ss make an

(1) high: self-esteem, not daunted by mistakes
(2) low: fossilization
5. Willingness to communicate: the intention to initiate communication, given a choice
self-confidence, self-esteem
6. Impulsive/ reflective
(1) reflective style: learns more effectively when they have time to consider new information
before responding •more accurate but slower in timed tests.
(2) impulsive style : learns more effectively when they can respond to new information
immediately 빠 risk takers, more willing to speak and better in timed tests, but less
accurate.
7. FI / FD
(1) Field-independent style
- distinguish parts from a whole (11• Selective listening )
learns most effectively when information is presented step-by-step and when new
―
words, structures or ideas are explained without reference to context.
- prefers bottom-up processing (begin with examining input to discover a generalization)
- grammatical analysis, attention to details, mastering of exercises, and mechanical drills.
(2) field-dependent
- perceive the whole picture.
learn best when information is presented in context
―
- prefer top-down processing (to begin with a prediction and apply it to particular
instances)
- learning involves communicative aspects: writing for specific communicative purposes,
and authentic reading text.
8. Visual, Auditory, Kinesthetic style

(2) Cognitive : thought processes used directly in dealing with the learning task

. Communication strategies

〔 〕
＃ Part2. Meth5dologies & Syl|abus
[A] Teaching methodologies
1. Audio-lingual method: a method of foreign or second language teaching which (a) emphasizes
the teaching of speaking and listening before reading and writing (b) uses Dialogues and Drills
(c) discourages use of the mother tongue in the classroom (d) often makes use of contrastive
analysis.
* Substitution drills : A teacher says a line and says a word or a phrase (called the cue) . The
students repeat the line the teacher has given them, substituting the cue into the line in its
proper place.
2, Total Physical Response: a language teaching method in which items are presented in the
foreign language as orders, commands, and instructions requiring a physical response from the
learner (e. g. opening a window or standing up) , TPR gives greater emphasis to comprehension
than many other teaching methods. Both this and the emphasis on teaching language through
physical activity are to lead to more effective learning,
3. Lexical Approach: an important part of learning a language consists of being able to
understand and produce lexical phrases as chunks. Students are thought to be able to perceive
patterns of language (grammar) as well as have meaningful set uses of words at their disposal
when they are taught in this way. Instruction focuses on fixed expressions that occur
frequently in dialogues
4. Task-based instruction: a teaching approach based on the use of communicative and
interactive tasks as the central units for the planning and delivery of instruction. Such tasks
are said to provide an effective basis for language learning since they: a) involve meaningful
communication and interaction (b) involve negotiation (c) enable the learners to acquire
grammar as a result of engaging in authentic language use.
5. Flipped learning: that which is traditionally done in class is now done at home, and that
which is traditionally done as homework is now completed in class. Direct instruction is
delivered to the individual outside of class (usually through videos) , and more strategic use of
in-class time for group work and individualized attention.
[B] Syllabus
1. Synthetic Syllabus
- learn language one thing at a time • gradual accumulation
- learning is additive, linear process (atomistic approach)
- acquisition : a process of gradual accumulation of parts until the whole structure of
language has been built up
Part 2. Methodologies & Syllabus 423

- Grammatical syllabus, notional/functional syllabus
* Notional syllabus: A syllabus in which the language content is arranged according to the
meanings a learner needs to express through language and the functions the learner will
use the language for. Notions(e. g. time, quantity, duration, location) and
functions(e. g_ requesting, suggesting, promising, describing) are used to develop learning
teaching units in a language course.
2. Analytic Syllabus
- language can be learned holistically, in chunks
- language from the use of experiential, communicative purpose
- language is not a subject in its own right, but merely a tool for communicating about
something else (not linguistically graded)
― Task-based syllabus, content-based syllabus
3. Product-oriented syllabus
- focus on the knowledge and skills which Ss should gain as a result of the instruction
- focus on the graded items, typically grammar-based, that learners are expected to know
before moving on to the following level.
- A typical product-oriented syllabus is the structural(grammatical) syllabus, based on the
grammatical structures graded according to complexity, which form the basis of the
grammar translation and audiolingual methods, as well as more "modern", such as Asher's
Total Physical Response and Gattengo's The Silent Way,
4. Process-oriented syllabus
- focus on the learning experiences themselves
- based on the pedagogic processes learners follow to reach their learning goals as per recent
developments in applied linguistics.

2. Dialogue
―
―  interpersonal:  to establish and maintain cordial social relationships (person-oriented)
- transactional:  to convey factual or propositional information (message oriented)
[C]  Authentic material
the  use of materials that  were not originally developed for pedagogical purposes,  such  as the
use  of  magazines,  newspapers,  advertisements,  news  reports,  or  songs,  Such  materials  are
often  thought  to  contain  more  realistic  and  natural  examples  of  language  use  than  those
found in textbooks and other specially developed teaching materials.

[A]
|     | Top-down VS    |     | Bottom-up  | processing  |     |     |     |
| --- | -------------- | --- | ---------- | ----------- | --- | --- | --- |
|     | 1.  Bottom-up  |     |            |             |     |     |     |
- decode(perceive)  individual sounds and words  in the  message  to derive meaning.
- focus  on  linguistic  information  more  intensively  (specific  word,  key  words,  key
transitions .. .)
|     | 2.  Top-down  |     |     |     |     |     |     |
| --- | ------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
- use background knowledge,  the situation,  context,  and topic to arrive at comprehension
- the  use  of prior knowledge  assists  listeners  in constructing  interpretations  and  facilitates
quicker processing.

| --- | --- | -------- | --------------------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
- Content  Schema:  include  general  world  knowledge,  sociocultural  knowledge,  and  topic
knowledge
- Formal schema :  consists of the knowledge we  have of the overall structure of some speech
events.  (background  knowledge  about  organizational  forms  and  discourse,  rhetorical
structures)

기출분석집
Cf) Metacognitive Instruction in Listening
- Metacognition includes elements of monitoring comprehension while listening and
evaluating the success of comprehension after listening
- T should promote the acquisition of metacognitive strategies : planning, monitoring, and
evaluating
- T can allows them to consciously intervene in the process by deploying efficient
strategies .
[B] Intensive listening / extensive listening
1. Intensive listening
- listen carefully in order to work on listening skills
- go over a piece of material multiple times and often mine it for other purposes such as
for dictation to build vocabulary and grammar
2 . Extensive listening
- listen for pleasure, listen to large amount of easy material (not trying to get all the
details. )
- listen for meaning with other development(e. g. new voca) , which occurs incidentally (does
not interrupt the focus on getting meaning 몐 교사가 자막과 함께 영 화 보여 주기
[CJ focus-on-form in listening
- Once Ss understood the meaning • Focus-on-Form activities that allow them to notice
the way things are being said
- Paying attention to the actual language and pronunciation (Phonemic distinctions,
intonation, stress) ex. listen again and check the words that are not stressed
- This changes the input they've received from listening into conscious intake
【4】 Listening Activities / Strategies
1. listening cloze: require the test-taker to listen to a story, monologue, or conversation and
simultaneously read the written text in which selected words or phrases have been deleted.
2. information transfer: involves getting students to put spoken or written texts into another
form, such as a chart, grid, picture, table or diagram - or vice-versa. These tasks encourage
deep processing of information
3. jigsaw listening
4. note-taking: while listening, the writing down of main points, reactions, questions, or other
responses. Such notes may be used for discussion or to help organize and retain information.
Part 3, Teaching Listening 427

__da\ EMillt:_ _____________________
5. listening for gist/details/ inferences
一
* inferencing - listen for meaning that is given or implied, but not stated directly.
• It helps Ss practice the deeper level understanding skill
6. Metacognitive strategies
(1) Predicting
- Ss think about the task they'll attempt
- function
a) Schema activation: help Ss develop a schema that they can apply to a subsequent
listening task
b) Task awareness : Ss think about the task they'll attempt • the quality and depth of
listening is enhanced when Ss have foresight to what the listening is likely to
contain
(2) Monitoring
- a strategy for checking the progress in the course of learning or carrying out a learning
task.
- check the accuracy of what is understood and compare with new information
(3) Evaluating
(4) Selective listening: scan the material selectively for certain information in the field of
distracting information • require Field independence
cf) Purpose of pre-listening
1) provide a context for interpretation
2) activate the background knowledge which will help interpretation
3) create motivation for listening (give purpose for listening)

- a  theory  of  reading  comprehension  that  sees  reading  as  involving  both  the  accurate  and

- begin reading by using TD until there is  a  problem  •  then shift  to BU  strategies

Summary
【4】 Reading Strategies
[A] Reading strategies
1. Skimming: skim for the main idea from a passage
2. Scanning: reading for a particular piece of information without necessarily understanding the
rest of a text
3. Inferencing: identify ideas that are not explicitly stated • look for the clues in the text and
use these clues to guess about the text and about the writer's ideas.
4. Using graphic organizer
- A visual representation to show relationship among ideas in a text.
- Students are able to see the key information in a text, the organization of text
information, the ways that information is structured, and the relationships among ideas
presented in a text.
5. Outlining: distinguish and list the main ideas and supporting details of a text• gives
readers an organized representation of the information in the text.
6. Critical reading: reader reacts critically to the text through relating the content of the
reading to personal standards, values, attitudes, and belief. The reader focuses on the
analysis of underlying textual ideologies and cultural messages.
[B] Strategy teaching
1. metacognitive awareness
- the learning process where learners become aware of their own mental processes which are
430 3. 부 록

홉
-
도
기출분석집
involved in different kinds of learning.
- Before reading • Ss plan how to successfully accomplish their reading goal • they select
which strategies to use for a particular reading task • begin a reading task • monitor
their own strategy use • ask themselves whether the strategies they are using are
effective.
2. Think-aloud protocol
T encourages Ss to monitor their comprehension process while reading. At the same time,
they are required to report on what they are doing and their thought to the teacher. • Ss
can verify strategies that assist them in getting at the meaning of what they are reading,
Part 4. Teaching Reading 431

〕
[ ＃ Part5. Teaching speaking&Pronunciation
【1】 Principles of teaching speaking
[A] Accuracy vs Fluency-based
1. Accuracy : language-oriented (teaching language 'usage')
- the extent to which Ss' speech matches what people actually say when they use target
language
- selecting the correct words and expressions to convey the intended meaning, as well as
using the grammatical patterns of English
2. Fluency: message-oriented (teaching language 'use')
- the extent to which Ss use the language quickly and confidently with few hesitations or
unnatural pauses, false start, word searches.
― natural language use take place when speaking activities focus on meaning and its
negotiation, when speaking strategies are used, and when overt correction is minimized.
- Fluency development activity : focus on meaning and communication of a message without
any particular focus on language features. • use language items within the previous
experience/ familiar topic 몐 4/3/2 technique
3. Complexity: the ability to use a wide and varied range of sophisticated structures and
vocabulary in the second language
[B] Interactional/ Transactional speech
- Transactional : for the purpose of conveying or exchanging specific information.
― Interpersonal : for the purpose of maintaining social relationships.
[C] Negotiation of meaning
Speaking activities with differences in pictures, opinions, or answers are ideal • more to
talk about • more communication breakdown • require negotiation of meaning • push Ss
to practice target language as much as possible
[D] Speech acts (function)
Ss should learn the appropriate ways to accomplish their goals when they are speaking •
This includes thanking, requesting information, apologizing, refusing, warning,
complimenting, directing, complaining and so on. • different forms can be used to
432 3. 부 록

홉
노 기출분석집
- - - - - - - - - - - - -- - - - - - - - - -
*
accomplish the same speech act.
Role play is the ideal way for practicing this language function, which allow students to
try out appropriate English utterances in potentially difficult situations, before they must use
them in real life.
[E]
Personalization
allow Ss to apply something they have learned to their own lives and express their own
ideas, feelings, preferences, and opinions
【2】 Speaking Activities
1. Gap activity
(1) I nformation gap
- two or more Ss have access to different information that they have to share in order to
complete the task • more negotiation of meaning (more comprehension/confirmation
check)
- Advantages
a) work well with Ss at most levels of proficiency from post beginners to advanced
b) Ss participate actively
c) All Ss have to take part if the task is to work
몐 pair work in which each member of the pair has a part of the total information (for
example an incomplete picture) and attempts to convey it verbally to the other.
(2) Reasoning gap: derive some new information from given information through process of
inference, deduction, practical reasoning, or perception of relationships
몐 deciding what course of action is best (for example cheapest or quickest) for a given
purpose and within given constraints. j(
(3) Opinion gap: identifying/articulating a personal preference, feeling, or attitude in response
to a given situation (negotiation of meaning
몐 taking part in the discussion of a social issue.
2. Jigsaw
Ss have different information that is needed to put together for the solution to a task.
• two-way, required, convergent, closed
3. Role-play
- based on real life situations
- Ss take on variety of roles • practice language which varies according to the setting, the
Part 5. Teaching Speaking&Pronunciation 433

__ff MMUtlt___ _____________________
formality of the situation, the degree of politeness, and function required for the particular
role (e. g, persuade, complain, invite ... )
- Work well when dealing with 'use' (selecting the righ t structure or form for a particular
con text) • T can alter the context • Ss practice how changes in the social variables affect
the choice of form.
4. Simulation: "reality of function in a simulated and structured environment"
― Reality of function : Ss must mentally accept the function the simulation requires of them •
Ss step inside the function mentally/behaviorally • do the best Ss can to carry out their
duties/responsibilities
- A simulated environment : as realistic as possible • creates an ideal context for taking
resonable risks
- explicit structure : explicitly structured around some reality problem • T provides the
conditions and the context for Ss
5. Problem-solving: Ss are given a problem and a set of information • must arrive at a
solution to the problem (generally, a single solution of the outcome)
6. Decision-making
【3】 Awareness-raising activities
[A] Noticing the gap
1. Noticing the gap
- refers to the learner realizing that the way he is saying something in the target language
differs from the way native speakers say it
- This awareness can be about individual words, grammar, idioms, phrases, pronunciation ...
2. Noticing the gap activity
(1) aimed at raising awareness of the difference between the learner's current competence and
the target competence
(2) procedures
- perform: 'having a go' using whatever linguistic means Ss have available
- observe: observe a skilled practitioner performing the same task
― re-performance: Ss incorporate the features they have learned
434 3. 부 록

흡
노 71출분석집
cf) consciousness-raising: promote noticing and continued awareness of the target form
- situate the grammar structures and elements in question with a broader discourse
context
— greater attention is paid to form-function relationship
【4】 Teaching Conversation
1. Indirect approach : Ss acquire conversation competence, peripherally, by engaging in
meaningful task
2_ Direct approach: plan a conversational program that explicitly draws Ss' attention to rules,
conventions, and strategies
b)
a) opening and closing
adjacency pairs : given to exchanges where a turn by one speaker requires an immediate
response as in greetings, invitations, compliments and complaint
c) turn taking
d) topic management
【5】 Teaching Pronunciation
[A] Features of pronunciation
1. Segments: vowels and consonants
2. Suprasegmental(prosody) : aspects of pronunciation which span more than one segment or
which are the property of stretches of speech rather than individual sounds • stress,
intonation, length, tone // chunks, prominence, linking ...
[BJ Principles - Bottom-up, Top-down, Balanced view
1. Bottom-up: segmentals, accuracy, practicing minimal pairs, word level, one sound at a time
2. Top-down: suprasegmentals, fluency, discourse level (in the stream of speech)
3. Balanced view: BU + TD, intelligibility (with an accent not distracting to the listener)
[C] Techniques
1. Minimal pairs
(1) Behavioristic: distinguish the minimal pairs as isolated words (with pictures)
Part 5. Teaching Speaking&Pronunciation 435

__fi +MiiAt_ ______________________
(2) Communicative : contextualized
2. Drills
(1) mechanical - no implied ccoonnnneeccttioonn wwitthh rreeaallittyy ((mmeeaanninngglleessss)),, ccoommpprreehheennssioonn is not
i i i i i i
required
(2) meaningful - ccoonnnneecctteedd ttoo ssoommee ff’ oorrmm ooff reality, understanding is required.
(3)
communicative - open response, communicative reality (conveying meaning in the real
word)
cf) - Repetition drill: Students are asked to repeat the teacher's model as accurately and as
quickly as possible.
- Substitution drill : A teacher says a line and says a word or a phrase (called the cue) . The
students repeat the line the teacher has given them, substituting the cue into the line in
its proper place.
- Chain drill: The teacher begins the chain by greeting particular student or asking him a
question. That student responds, then turns to the student sitting next to him. The first
student greets or asks a question of the second student and the chain continues.
- Transformation drill: The teacher gives student a certain kind of sentence pattern and
students are asked to transform this sentence (for example, from an affirmative sentence
to negative sentence. / changing a statement into a question)
436 3. 부 록

홉

. .... ...... ....
[ ＃ Part6. Teaching writing 〕
【1】 Product-oriented approach
[A] Product-oriented approach
- focus on the completed written product, the coherent, error-free text
- T provides 'model composition' • Ss imitate, copy, and transform models • try to meet a
list of criteria (content, organization, vocabulary, grammar, mechanism)
【2】 Process-oriented approach
[A] Characteristics
- go through stages of drafting and receiving feedback on their draft from peers/T, followed
by rreevv
i
iss
i
i ons of their evolving texts.
- Advantages
a) Ss discover their own voice b) engages Ss in meaningful writing
c) give writers a sense of audience d) provide formative feedback through conferencing
[BJ Steps
1. Pre-writing
(1) Brainstorming: Ss think quickly without inhibition to produce as many ideas as possible on
a given topic.
(2) freewriting: in a given time, Ss keep writing without stopping and concerning for spelling,
grammar, or punctuation.
(3) Listing: Ss produce as lengthy a list as possible of all the main ideas and subcategories
that come to mind as they think about the topic at hand.
(4) clustering: a key word placed in a center of a page • around which Ss quickly jot down
all of the free-associations triggered by the subject matter, using words or short phrases.
Ss share • exposed to a wide variety of approaches to the subjects
2. Drafting
- Ss focus on fluency/ the development of ideas, the organization of those ideas
- visualize an audience • let Ss have a real-world purpose (authentic audience)
Ss have a conscious sense of audience • Ss write more clearly, actively
3. Feedback (Responding)
Part 6. Teaching Writing 437

- From T: conference • give formative feedback on the ideas and organization rather than
on grammar, spelling
- From peers: peer feedback must be modeled • provide structured feedback form (a list of
directed Qs, checklist)
- collaborative learning • sharpen their critical skills in analyzing both peer's work and
their own drafts
4. Revising (Redrafting, Proofreading)
- Ss review on the basis of the feedback given in feedback stage
- Ss improve global content and organization of ideas + check language error
- Ss work in pairs to read aloud each other's draft • bring more conscious level of
rethinking what they've written.
- A checklist can be given : 몐 Are the verb forms correct?
【3】 Web-based Instruction
1. Computer assisted language learning (CALL) : the uussee ooff a computer in the teaching or
learning of a second or foreign language, CALL may take the form of: (a) a activities which
parallel learning through other media but which use the facilities of the computer (e. g, using
the computer to present a reading text) , (b) activities which are extensions or adaptations of
print-based or classroom based activities (e. g. computer programs that teach writing skills by
help ing the student develop a topic and thesis statement and by checking a composition for
vocabulary, grammar, and topic development), (c) activities which are unique to CALL.
2 . Synchronous vs Asynchronous
(1) Synchronous: communication that is instantaneous, with all participants logged onto their
computers and sending messages in real time. Language classes often use this type of
(2) communication in the form of Internet chat.
Asynchronous: communication that is not instantaneous and can be accessed and read by
the recipient at a later time. Language classes often use this type of communication in the
form of bulletin boards or discussion lists.
3. Corpora and concordancing
- a list of all the words which are used in a particular text or in the works of a particular
author, together with a list of the contexts in which each word occurs (usually not
including highly frequent grammatical words such as articles and prepositions) .
Concordances have been used in the study of word frequencies, grammar, discourse and
stylistics
438 3. 부 록

기출분석집
- Data-driven approach : responsibility is placed on the learner to make their own
comparisons of the way in which target expressions are used and thus to formulate rules
on their own.
【4】 Teacher's Feedback
[A] Oral feedback - conferencing
1. Conference on Ss' draft: One-on-one conversation between T and S • T facilitates the
improvement of the written work (draft) • direct feedback toward a student's specific needs •
directly ask Ss about intended message which are often difficult to decipher + uncover
potential misunderstanding Ss might have about prior written feedback or issues discussed in
class
cf) T's feedback on Ss' draft
- Ss' first draft • indicate major error, comment on thesis, organization, coherence
- Ss' second draft • indicate minor error, mechanical error, cohesive device
[BJ Written feedback Q
- Essay Correction code
cf) Cohesion and Coherence
*
Coherence
- the relationship which links the meaning of utterances in a discourse or of the sentences
in a text (following the sequence of ideas and points)
— Text coherence depends upon :
1) knowledge of the structure of the text genre (e. g_ understanding that a story consists
of a setting, plot, and resolution)
2) ability to focus on the causal or temporal chain of events in a narrative text and on
the phenomena described and explained in an expository text.
* Cohesion
- "Text forming devices" • the grammatical and/or lexical relationship between the
different elements of a text (linguistic ways of connecting ideas across sentences)
- types
1) Reference: personal (he), demonstrative (this, that), comparative(this one? • no 'the
other')
2) Substitution (one do, so) and Ellipsis (Both ( ) are. Yes I have ( ) )
3) Coonnjj unction : However And, Then, because
4) Lexical cohesion: repetition, synonym, general word (things), Superordinate
(Pneumonia • Illness)
Part 6. Teaching Writing 439

【5】 Types / activities
[A] Controlled/ Guided /Free Writing
1. Controlled writing: focuses on specific features of the written language such as grammar,
vocabulary, syntax, and conventions of written language (punctuation, connecting words,
spelling)
2. Guided writing: concerns ideas and facts supplied by T for Ss to express in their own
sentences (T loosens control • gives some but not all of the content/form)
* T gives an outline, information to include, Qs to respond to help Ss formulate their
ideas
3. Free writing: T gives only the title and Ss themselves decide on the facts they should include
and their arrangement and expression in some logical sequence
□ Controlled (production of accurate language in context) • Guided (the organization of
material which is given) • Free (production by Ss of both content and language)
Cf) Dicto-comp (controlled writing • a paragraph is read two or three times and Ss rewrite the
paragraph to the best of their recollection of the reading)
[B] Self-writing
1. Dialogue journal
enable Ss and T to interact on a one-to-one basis at any level in any learning content Ss
can generate some personal input and receive the T's direct feedback • fluency i ,
interpersonal communication
2. Note-taking, Diary
[C] Display writing/ Real writing
1. Display writing: when the instructor may be the only reader and is only interested in
assessing the students' knowledge, (written excercise, short answer essays, writing in test
situations)
2. Real writing: when the reader genuinely wants the information. （멘 writing letters with the
authentic reader in mind)

〔
〕
＃ Part7. Teaching Grammar
〈Form-Focused Instruction (FFI)〉
Form-focused Instruction : any planned or incidental instructional activity that is intended to
induce language learners to pay attention to linguistic form. (include focus on form, focus on
forms, error correction, negotiation of form ....)

v
lmut > lrtake lnter1angLege system output ― ��]
(implicit L2 knowledge)
t
【1】 Comprehension-based(Processing)
aracteristics
[A] Ch
- Grammar instruction intervention takes place at the input stage when Ss are actively
engaged in comprehension ( instruction involves manipulation of input)
- The learners are induced to notice the target grammar point and its features as they try
to comprehend oral and written texts
- acquisition occurs when learners attend to the new structure in input • Ss do not involve
actually producing the target structure
[B] Activities
1. Input processing
- Input processing: push Ss to attend to properties of language during activities where the
structures is being used meaningfully
- Characteristics
1) Ss are given info. about a linguistic form
2) S••s are pushed to process the form while attending only to meaning
”
with "� ( input that is manipulated in particular ways to push Ss to
become dependent on form to get meaning) • Ss more efficiently extract meaning from
Part 7. Teaching Grammar 441

__ff iii FIA _ _____________________
non-salient features, such as functions, inflections, and syntactic features.
3) Ss will be able to make better form-meaning connections (Ss see how grammar assists
in the delivery of that message)
4) Ss do NOT respond by producing the target form
2. Input enhancement
- techniques for getting Ss to notice the target grammar item
- keep Ss' attention on meaning
- make target items more salient through underlining, boldfacing, italicizing, coloring ...
3. Input flooding
- Ss are provided with numerous examples of a certain target form in the input.
- Frequent instances of the same target form • likely to be noticed • draw Ss' attention to
form
【2】 Explicit instruction
[A] Cha racteristics
- Form intervention is intended to impinge on the Ss' 12 knowledge by deliberately directing
them to attend to form (instruction helps Ss develop explicit knowledge of target
structure)
- Types
a) Direct (deductive) : metalinguistic descriptions of the target feature • controlled
exercise
b) Indirect (inductive) : Ss are provided with data illustrating the target feature •
required to discover the rule for themselves 몐 CR task
[B] Activities
1. CR task: draw Ss' attention to a particular linguistic feature through a range of inductive and
deductive procedures. (Explicit instruction • indirect, inductive, discovery learning)
(1) procedures
- T prepare data containing exemplars of the targeted feature
- Ss operate on the data using their intellectual effort to understand the target features
— Ss verbalize a rule with their own linguistic resources
- T provide explicit rules
(2) benefits
- help Ss incorporate the target feature into their interlanguage
- What Ss find out for themselves is better remembered than what they are simply told
- Ss are NOT required to produce the target form • Instead, they participate in
442 3. 부 록

71출분석집
'meta-talk' (discuss how language system works)
cf) Garden path
- a strategy for correcting oral errors linked t o overgeneralization of target language rules
― Give Ss only a partial explanation • Get Ss to overgeneralize • leads them into error
• Ss modify their hypothesis • Ss process the target structure somewhat more deeply
- Ss are more likely to learn the exceptions to the rule if they are corrected at the
moment the overgeneralization error is made
【3】 Production practice
[A] Cha racteristics
- Form intervention takes place at the output stage through tasks that include language
production (• structure-based production tasks)
- induce Ss to produce utterances containing the pre-selected target structure
[B] Activities
1. Dictogloss
(1) Procedure
a. Preparation: T prepares Ss for the text by asking Qs and discussing pie, voca, and by
ensuring Ss know what they will do. (usually, the text includes the TARGET FORM)
b. Dictation: Ss hear the dictation twice ( for general feeling and + for note taking)
c. Reconstruction: Ss in groups, pool notes and produce their own version of the text (T
doesn't provide language input • Ss generate not an exact replica but to reproduce its
content)
d. Analysis & correction : Ss analysis the reconstructed text • check grammar & compare
their version with original one.
(2) Features
- Focus-on-form activity: meaningful negotiation + draw attention to linguistic form
- Reconstruction • Ss engage in 'meta-talk' (talk about language) in order to know
whether sentences are grammatically correct • raise Ss' consciousness of specific aspects
of language use. • Compare theirs with a correct version • Noticing the gap (Ss notice
the differences between their own interlanguage and Target language) • foster grammar
acquisition
- it raises Ss' consciousness about the working of grammar in context • reinforce form­
function relationship
Part 7. Teaching Grammar 443

【4】 Related concepts in teaching Grammar
[A] Deductive vs Inductive
1. Deductive
(1) T gives a grammatical explanation or rule followed by a set of exercise designed to clarify
the grammar point and help Ss master the point
(2) Advantage: time-saving • more time for practice and application
(3) Disadvantage : frruussttrraattee SSss who don't have sufficient metalanguage/ less memorable/
T-centered
2. Inductive
(1) present Ss with samples of language and through a process of guided discovery, get them
to work out the principle or rule for themselves
(2) Advantage
- Rules Ss discover for themselves fit their existing mental structures more
meaningful, memorable
- The mental effort • greater cognitive depth • greater memorability
- Ss are more likely to participate actively, attentively, and motivatively.
- greater self-reliance and autonomy.
(3) Disadvantage: more time and energy, less productive practice, Ss may hypothesize the
wrong rule.
[B] Procedural vs declarative knowledge
1. Declarative knowledge: Know 'about' the knowledge • ability to articulate complex rules
governing language (conscious)
2. Procedural knowledge: Acquire linguistic patterns without explicit instruction and use them in
language task automatically and spontaneously. know things without being aware of the
structure (unconscious)
3. Proceduralization of declarative knowledge A great deal of meaningful practice + T's
feedback on the accuracy of the target form
(1) require practice in which Ss use the target form meaningfully while keeping the declarative
(2)
knowledge in working memory (form + meaning-oriented)
meaningful practice of pattern • Ss interact in a way that is meaningful to them (they
say something meaningful) • high motivation, more attentive
(3) avoid meaningless mechanical drill (it does not engage Ss in the target behavior of
conveying meaning through language • harder to retrieve in the context of communicative
interaction)
444 3. 부 록

홉
노
기출분석집
[CJ Focus-on-form VS Focus-on-forms
1. Focus on Form
- Draw Ss' attention to linguistic forms within the context of meaning-focused activities
- Communication first • focus on form second
― Ss are more likely to see the relationship between language form and communicative
function
2. Focus on Forms
- synthetic syllabus • language consists of a series of grammatical forms that can be
acquired additively / sequentially, • teaching linguistic form, but communicative use X
[D] F
orm-meaning mappi
i
nngg(( connection) a빠
- link between a lexical or grammatical form and its meaning the connection that the
learner's internal processors make between referential real-word meaning and how that
meaning is encoded linguistically.
몐 connection between "the inflectional suffix /-ed/" and "past time reference"
- example activities : Structured-input activities (for 'past tense') , Use of pictures or realia
(for 'comparatives'), Using Actions (for 'prepositions')
[E] Consciousness-raising
- The way that Ss become aware of features of the language they are learning. (promote
빠
noticing and continued awareness of the target language)
- helps Ss 'notice' the features of the input (input • notice • intake) active role of Ss
- activities are structured in such a way as to invite Ss to develop their own hypothesis
about the targeted feature of the language
- How?
a. make certain items more salient (input enhancement)
b. ask Ss to infer rules from examples (inductive learning)
c. compare Ss' own output with that of more proficient users of target language (noticing
the gap)
d. pushed output, where they are forced to notice the holes in the present stage of their
language knowledge (output hypothesis)

【2】 Strategies, Techniques
[A] Guueessssing strategies - from context or b y using word part knowledge
i
- The best internalization of vocabulary comes form encounters with words within the
context
- Decide the part of speech • Examine its immediate context • Look at the relationship
between this clause or sentence and other sentences or paragraphs • look at the
rhetorical relationship, punctuation, and reference words • use knowledge gained from
such clues to guess the meaning of the word
[B] M
nemonic devices
- Keyword method: helps link a word form and its meaning and to consolidate this linkage
in memory (use similarity between Ll and L2 + use visual image)
[C] Word map, Semantic mapping
- Semantic mapping: helps bring into consciousness relationship among words in a text and
helps deepen understanding by creating associative networks for words
[D] C oncordancing
(1) A Bottom-up Study of text (Data-driven)
(2) arrays the occurrences of a given lexical items of expressions from a corpus of language
data in context • allow for comparison of the usage of the item or expression
(3) Advantages
- Ss can closely examine associations between grammar and lexical units
- It creates list of collocations
- Ss can look at the context in which a given word or phrase occurs
- Ss can have fine-tuning word senses (get different sense of meaning among the
synonymous)
- It shows the frequency of the words • help T identify and prioritize vocabulary words
that need to be taught
Cf) Paradigmatic / Syntagmatic relation
*
paradigmatic relation: reflects thee sseemmaanntticc cchhooices at a particular structure point in a
i i
sentence • antonymy, synonymy, ..
syntagmatic rreellaatt ion : related to syntactic relationship • collocation
[E] Using Realia
- actual objects and items which are brought into a classroom as examples or as aids to be talked
or written about and used in teaching. Realia may include such things as photographs, articles of
clothing, and kitchen objects.
